perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall, and shall cause its Affiliates and Permitted Subcontractors to, maintain records in sufficient detail for the other Party to confirm compliance with this Agreement and in good scientific manner appropriate for patent and regulatory purposes under Applicable Law, which shall [***] properly reflect all activities conducted and results achieved by such Party under this Agreement. Such records shall be retained by such Party, its Affiliates or Permitted Subcontractors until [***] ([***]) years after the end of the period to which such books and records pertain or for such longer period as may be required by Applicable Law.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall use reasonable efforts to cause its Affiliates and Permitted Subcontractors to maintain records with some level of detail for the other Party to generally understand compliance with this Agreement and in a manner that seems appropriate for patent and regulatory purposes under what they believe to be Applicable Law, which will vaguely reflect activities conducted and results achieved by such Party under this Agreement. Such records may be retained by such Party, its Affiliates or Permitted Subcontractors until what they consider to be an appropriate time after the end of the period to which such books and records pertain.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity by replacing 'maintain records in sufficient detail' with 'maintain records with some level of detail,' weakening the standard for record-keeping. Replacing 'good scientific manner appropriate for patent and regulatory purposes under Applicable Law' with 'a manner that seems appropriate for patent and regulatory purposes under what they believe to be Applicable Law' introduces subjectivity. Replacing 'shall be retained' with 'may be retained' makes record retention discretionary. This overall weakening of the legal requirement undermines the ability to verify compliance and fulfill regulatory needs.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 58 - Good Laboratory Practice for Nonclinical Laboratory Studies
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 58.185 mandates that all data arising from or relating to a nonclinical laboratory study must be retained. The modified text's use of 'may be retained' and the introduction of subjective criteria for record-keeping directly contradict this mandatory requirement for retaining all study data, potentially hindering regulatory oversight and compliance.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and discretion in record-keeping, directly contradicting the mandatory requirements for data retention in nonclinical laboratory studies. The original text required parties to 'maintain records in sufficient detail' and retain them for a specified period or as required by law. The modified text weakens this requirement by stating parties should use 'reasonable efforts to cause its Affiliates and Permitted Subcontractors to maintain records with some level of detail,' making record retention discretionary, and introducing subjectivity by saying that records 'may be retained'. According to the Law Explanation, 21 CFR § 58.185 mandates retaining all data from nonclinical laboratory studies, and the modified text contradicts this.

perturbation.original_text: BLI shall (a) use qualified personnel in connection with performing Services for Ginkgo and perform such Services in a competent and workmanlike manner consistent with prevailing industry standards and in material conformance with Applicable Laws, the terms of this Agreement (which shall include the terms of any Workflow Development Plans) and (b) obtain and maintain all material licenses, permits or approvals required by Applicable Laws, the terms of this Agreement (which shall include the terms of any approved Workflow Development Plans) in connection with performing Services for Ginkgo.
perturbation.changed_text: BLI shall endeavor to use personnel it generally considers to be qualified when performing Services for Ginkgo and perform such Services in a manner it reasonably believes to be competent and workmanlike, and in substantial conformance with what it understands to be Applicable Laws and the terms of this Agreement (which shall generally include the terms of any Workflow Development Plans) and may attempt to obtain and maintain licenses, permits or approvals it thinks are required by what it perceives to be Applicable Laws and the terms of this Agreement (which may include the terms of any approved Workflow Development Plans) when performing Services for Ginkgo.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'use qualified personnel' with 'endeavor to use personnel it generally considers to be qualified,' making the qualification standard subjective and discretionary. The phrase 'perform such Services in a competent and workmanlike manner consistent with prevailing industry standards and in material conformance with Applicable Laws' is weakened by replacing it with 'perform such Services in a manner it reasonably believes to be competent and workmanlike, and in substantial conformance with what it understands to be Applicable Laws,' introducing subjectivity and uncertainty regarding the applicable standards and laws. Replacing 'obtain and maintain all material licenses, permits or approvals required by Applicable Laws' with 'may attempt to obtain and maintain licenses, permits or approvals it thinks are required by what it perceives to be Applicable Laws' makes compliance with legal requirements discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: CA Bus. & Prof. Code § 7068 requires that a business must have a qualified person responsible for direct supervision and control of construction operations. The modification weakens the obligation to use 'qualified personnel,' potentially violating this requirement for competent supervision and control, which is crucial for regulatory compliance in California. This is especially relevant if the 'Services' provided involve construction or related activities that require specific licenses and expertise.

Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 5. Licensing [7065 - 7077] ( Article 5 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) 7068. (a) The board shall require an applicant to show the degree of knowledge and experience in the classification applied for, and the general knowledge of the building, safety, health, and lien laws of the state and of the administrative principles of the contracting business that the board deems necessary for the safety and protection of the public. (b) An applicant shall qualify in regard to their experience and knowledge in one of the following ways: (1) If an individual, they shall qualify by personal appearance or by the appearance of their responsible managing employee who is qualified for the same license classification as the classification being applied for. (2) If a partnership or a limited partnership, it shall qualify by the appearance of a general partner or by the appearance of a responsible managing employee who is qualified for the same license classification as the classification being applied for. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Page Not Found - CSLB -CSLB You have found a page that no longer exists. You will be taken to the CSLB homepage momentarily.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the requirement for qualified personnel and adherence to applicable laws, potentially contradicting CA Bus. & Prof. Code § 7068, which mandates demonstrating knowledge and experience for applicant depending on classification. The original text mandates specific actions, whereas the modified text introduces subjectivity ('endeavor,' 'reasonably believes,' 'thinks,' 'perceives') and discretion, making compliance less certain. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the need to ensure 'the safety and protection of the public' which the modifications undermine. Snippet 2, while a 404, does not negate Snippet 1.
